data.title: Kevin Warsh Will Not Be The Fed Chair Trump Expects
data.description:
Introduction

The next Chairman of the Federal Reserve has an unenviable job ahead of him. The current economic climate seems mildly stagflationary, which could be exacerbated by both AI’s threat to the job market (a deflationary effect) and the supply shocks due to the War in Iran (an inflationary effect). In my view the numbers and metrics belie the real state of the economy. As Goodhart’s Law predicts, when a metric becomes a target, it ceases to be a good metric. Even though the metrics may tell a good story, the ‘economy’ is more intangible, and Americans tend to feel like it remains in a weak state. Although GDP growth has been positive since 2020, and the US has not slipped into a recession, other metrics like credit card and auto loan delinquencies, consumer confidence, and labor force participation tell a different story. Ignoring data center spending, GDP growth was only 0.1% in the first half of 2025.

In this article, I lay out what I see as the minefield of potential problems lying in wait for the next Fed Chair, what types of attributes I believe are needed in a Fed Chair who can navigate this minefield, and whether Kevin Warsh is that man or not. Finally, I explain what I think it means for equity markets and the S&P 500.

The Landscape of Problems

Inflation Stickiness

The last time the Consumer Price Index was under the Fed’s 2% target rate was in February of 2021. In every month since then the year-over-year CPI has been above the target rate. In March 2026, the CPI hit 3.3%, which was the highest reading since April 2024. Increased energy and food prices drove the CPI reading higher in March. Core CPI (ignoring food and energy costs) was lower, at 2.6%. Even if we accept the dogma that Core CPI is a better indicator than the overall CPI due to the latter’s inclusion of volatile food and energy prices, inflation is still above the Fed’s target.

The chart below shows how energy costs have dominated the inflation figure from March 2026:

Right now inflation metrics are going the opposite way that a Fed Chair would want. I think it’s fair to say that most of us would have expected inflation to have returned to the 2% baseline by now, over four years after the Fed initiated the fastest rate hikes in history. This means that the next Fed Chair will likely have limited options when it comes to pursuing dovish monetary policy.

Employment Still Weak

The US hiring rate fell to 3.1% in February 2026, which was the lowest it has been since April 2020. The “quits rate” is also an important indicator of perceived labor market strength. In financial activities, quits dropped from 116k a year ago to 104k this year. In professional and business services, quits went from 520k in February of 2025 to 468k in February of 2026. The chart below shows the total number of quits in white-collar job categories over the last year:

Additionally, total private hires are down from 4.9 million a year ago to 4.5 million in February 2026. Separations are similarly down. So we see an emerging pattern of people not quitting and companies not hiring or firing, leading to a stagnating labor market. As Gallup recently reported:

The data point to a workforce that is restless but largely stuck. Many workers who want to leave cite economic constraints — from pay and benefits to the difficulty of finding a comparable role — as the primary barriers to making a move. With life evaluation at a record low and job market confidence near historic lows, the conditions weighing on U.S. workers show few signs of easing.

I believe AI is part of the explanation for the weak labor market for a few reasons. First, companies are cutting workers to free up funds to invest in AI infrastructure. Second, as I covered in my recent article on Robert Half International, AI is allowing applicants to swamp job applications with AI-generated resumes and cover letters. That puts companies in a “Paradox of Choice” situation: there are so many applicants for every open role that companies can’t actually find the good ones. They deploy AI to screen resumes as well, but when there are 5000 applicants for a job and there’s little way to tell which resumes are genuine and which are AI-generated, it becomes impossible to filter through the massive stack of applicants. Third, AI is allowing current employees to do more with less, which means that when an employee quits or is let go, their work may just be absorbed by another employee using AI to be twice as productive. So companies may not be replacing displaced workers.

At this point it’s clear that AI is going to be disruptive to the labor market, particularly in white-collar roles. If we use AI correctly, we can absorb displaced workers into a larger economy, with new businesses blooming and old ones expanding. But individuals and companies are likely to wait until they can borrow at lower rates before committing to significant rehiring. In my view, monetary policy has to incentivize expansion to counteract the deflationary effects of AI on the labor market. On his argument that AI will create deflationary pressure in the economy that will allow for cutting rates, I think Warsh is on solid ground. But at the same time I think his plan to reduce the Fed's balance sheet could be an attempt to have his cake and eat it too. The Fed has to make the hard choice about whether labor market weakness outweighs sticky inflation and potential supply issues. If it does (which is my view), the Fed needs to get in front of it, rather than trying to have it both ways.

Further Supply Shocks

The closure of the Strait of Hormuz is creating a substantial supply shock for global markets. Supply shocks mixed with expansionary monetary policy are a recipe for disaster, as we learned in 1974 and 2022. It’s not just oil stuck in the Persian Gulf - it’s also helium, aluminum, sulfur, fertilizers, and more. Although we’re less dependent on the Gulf states than we were in the 1970s, the United States is still a price-taker in these commodity markets. That means US consumers still end up paying higher prices.

I’m a believer in Milton Friedman’s famous edict: “Inflation is always and everywhere a monetary phenomenon”. If the money supply isn’t being rapidly expanded, then higher prices in some goods or services may be offset by lower prices in other goods and services, so that the net effect is negligible. However, I do think that supply shocks can exacerbate monetary-driven inflation. And so relaxing monetary policy during significant supply-side shocks may be at best unrealistic.

Furthermore, tariffs create additional supply-related disruptions. Although the Supreme Court recently struck down President Trump’s prior set of imposed tariffs, there are no signs that the President is giving up on his ‘favorite word in the dictionary’ that easily. As I’ve covered in recent articles on Constellium SE and Century Aluminum, aluminum tariffs have pushed the “Midwest Premium” in aluminum prices up to the highest on record, at over $2400 per ton. Aluminum is not the only raw material made significantly more expensive by tariffs - a similar phenomenon exists in the tariffed steel, copper, and lumber markets. These higher raw materials costs make even domestically-manufactured finished goods more expensive, another inflationary effect that could tie the next Fed Chair’s hands.

So the next Fed Chairman has to walk a tightrope: a labor market that needs rate cuts and spurred investment to mitigate the devastation that AI promises to unleash while at the same time a sticky inflationary backdrop with more supply shocks yet to be felt in prices. It’s vital that the Fed’s policy anticipates the challenges that right now are off on the horizon but fast-approaching.

Where the “Data-Driven” Powell Fed Went Wrong

In my view the most potent critique of the Powell Fed relates to its insistence on using data as the primary way to inform decisions. The problem though is that data is necessarily backwards looking. The purpose of macroeconomic models is to predict how the economy will evolve in response to policy changes. It requires courage to trust one’s worldview and make a decision before the empirical evidence calls for it. But that is what is expected of people who get paid the big bucks and who occupy the most powerful jobs in the world.

On the one hand, an approach led by data can be extraordinarily useful. The data the Fed was receiving and relying on during the Covid crisis allowed them to react quickly and decisively. They relied on quick metrics like private payroll processing data, mobility data, and credit/debit card transactions to slash interest rates, which led to an extremely quick recovery from the pandemic relative to other economic crashes.

The data-driven approach also prevented a rate hike in the late-2010’s. Relying purely on theoretical models might have led the Fed to raise rates, but because the data didn’t show that lower unemployment was leading to higher inflation, the Fed didn’t react to declining unemployment, which allowed for significant labor market expansion.

But we learned the limits of the “data-driven approach” during the inflationary surge of 2021 and 2022. The problem is that the Fed’s policy changes don’t tend to take effect immediately. By the time a monetary expansion creates inflation that becomes apparent in data, it’s too late to quickly correct with policy. If the policy relies too heavily on data then it’s inherently reactive rather than prescriptive.

The lesson we should take away from this is that data can quickly alert us to acute economic or monetary conditions, but not chronic ones. I believe the ideal Fed Chairman is someone who both leans on real-time data and on an economic worldview. Real-time data can allow adaptive short-term responses while a coherent economic worldview can allow the Fed to look ahead 12-18 months and anticipate how policy today will be instantiated in the future economy.

So is Kevin Warsh the guy that can walk the tightrope? I believe that in some ways he is and in some ways he isn’t. Ironically, I don’t think he’s the best candidate to carry out President Trump’s preference of stimulative monetary policy. Warsh may pursue a lower Federal Funds rate but that doesn’t necessarily mean that he will pursue expansionary monetary policy.

“Rules-Based” Policy

Warsh believes that monetary policy should be “rules-based”, meaning it ties rate decisions to formulaic metrics like the Taylor Rule, developed by economist John Taylor in 1993. The Taylor rule says that the nominal federal funds rate should be a function of the equilibrium real interest rate, inflation rate, target inflation rate, and output gap. That kind of framework is in stark contrast to the discretionary nature of the Powell Fed. However, it’s also not making use of a model or worldview to predict the state of the economy in the long-run. In 2021, using the Taylor rule may not have helped much, since there was still a negative output gap in 2021 and the inflation had not hit the economy yet. So in 2021, when interest rates needed to be raised preemptively, the Taylor rule would have supported low rates (albeit not 0%). In early 2022, the Taylor Rule would have set the Federal Funds rate at nearly 13%, while the Fed still had rates at near-zero.

At present, the Taylor Rule would advocate a higher Federal Funds rate (in the 4-5.5% range). But like the “data-driven approach” of the current Fed, this “rules-based approach” takes account of the seas we’ve just sailed through rather than the seas we’re sailing into. The Taylor Rule cannot account for the impacts of AI on the job market in 12 or 24 months from now, and set policy to preempt those impacts. Similarly, it cannot decipher whether a Middle East supply shock is going to impact prices in a transient or long-lasting way. We need a Fed Chair who doesn’t just blindly implement what the data or rules suggest, but who can use good judgment to understand where the economy is headed and act in advance of it.

The benefit of a rules-based policy would be its greater immunity to political pressure. Policymakers have an incentive to prioritize short-term employment gains over long-term price stability. So if the Fed’s policy is viewed as rules-based rather than discretionary, it insulates the Fed from political pressure to enact expansionary monetary policy when it would be inappropriate to do so. The type of data a rules-based policy would rely on would be more real-time as well. Using bond market pricing allows for a “wisdom of the crowd” effect to pave the way for Fed policy. Also since bond yields are often leading indicators of recession or expansion, a rules-based policy could allow the Fed to enact policy before it’s needed, and potentially before it’s too late.

The weaknesses involve Goodhart’s law again. When everyone knows the metrics the Fed uses to create monetary policy, those metrics will likely cease to be good predictive indicators for the necessity of the policies they’re meant to call for. If the Federal Reserve is watching the bond market for signals, and the bond market is determining asset prices by watching the Fed watching the bond market, it creates a circularity that becomes mutually useless. The Fed and bond market together would be acting like a dog chasing its tail.

The other obvious problem with using bond yields to inform Fed policy is that markets aren’t always pricing assets rationally. If the Fed relies purely on a rules-based approach to policy, then it could confuse temporary asset volatility with true economic signals.

Smaller Balance Sheet & Lower Rates

The Fed currently has a $6.68 trillion balance sheet (this is after a long period of quantitative tightening). Warsh has explicitly stated that he wants the Federal Reserve to lighten the balance sheet. He also likely plans to cut the policy rate. Doing both would likely increase long-term yields while decreasing short-term yields, steepening the yield curve. This could be a double-edged sword. On the one hand, a steeper yield curve improves net interest margins for banks, which incentivizes them to lend more. But on the other hand, it makes it more expensive for companies to borrow money both privately and in the bond markets, since corporate debt is typically priced off the long end of the yield curve. So this policy change could be good for small and mid size businesses who raise capital directly through banks, but worse for larger companies who can borrow by issuing bonds.

In his hearing Warsh said he plans to shift the Fed’s focus from using the balance sheet as a policy tool toward greater reliance on the interest rate as a policy tool:

The Fed has an interest rate tool and a balance sheet tool, and my view is that the interest rate tool gets in the cracks, it’s fairer. The balance sheet tool, disproportionally, helps those with financial assets. The interest rate tool hits the entire economy. So we need a new framework, new tools.

Scrapping Forward Guidance

Warsh is not a believer in signaling future Fed policy. Since the financial crisis, the Fed has used forward guidance to reduce interest rate volatility. As Warsh said in his hearing:

I think part of the reason why after making a mistake in 2021 and 22, the mistake was compounded is the Fed gives its forward guidance. The Fed tells the whole world what their dots are going to be, what their forecasts are going to be. Well, the Fed's human. Then they hold onto those forecasts longer than they should. I think if the Fed were to wait until it gets into a meeting before making a decision, that incremental deliberation can keep the Central Bank from compounding its errors.

With a “rules-based” policy, Warsh is likely to make more impromptu changes to the Fed’s rate. This is meant to force private sector risk management, rather than give the private sector short-term certainty over the direction of rates. This change would likely benefit sophisticated investors who deliberately manage risks, while increasing risk for leveraged investors or rate-sensitive borrowers.

Who Wins and Who Loses?

I believe that the Federal Reserve under Kevin Warsh will likely not be as dovish as either investors or President Trump would like. The Fed’s balance sheet expansion has been favorable to asset holders, but not necessarily to small business owners. Decreasing the Fed’s balance sheet whilst simultaneously lowering the policy rate would create clear winners and losers. The winners would likely be banks and financial institutions, small-cap companies, Main Street businesses, and consumers with short-term or adjustable debt. Losers would be long-term bondholders, homebuyers, long-term corporate borrowers, and cash-savers (yields on money market accounts would decline). During testimony, Warsh agreed with this statement from Senator Bernie Moreno of Ohio:

One of the key parts of why I'm supporting you is because a total and complete focus and understanding that the interest rate tool is the tool that allows you to help working Americans much more than the balance sheet tool.

The expansion of the Fed's balance sheet is viewed by some as a policy tool that has exacerbated wealth inequality by artificially inflating the value of assets (therefore, those who own the most assets do the best when the Fed expands its balance sheet). Lowering the policy rate is then viewed as a tool that tilts toward the benefit of non-asset holders like borrowers and small business owners who depend on short-term credit.

A few categories of equities would likely be winners. First, as I mentioned, small-cap stocks rely more heavily on short-term funding and floating-rate debt than large-cap stocks do, so small-cap companies may see lower financing costs. Second, financial stocks could benefit through expansion of net interest margins. Third, tech stocks could benefit since lower short-term rates could reduce the cost of capital required for large technological investments. Fourth, utilities and dividend stocks could provide an outlet to money currently invested in money markets and short-term debt.

There would also be a few categories of losers. First, real estate/REITs: real estate is most sensitive to long-term interest rates and borrowing costs for long-term mortgages go up as the yield curve gets steeper. This would put pressure on both REITs and property values in my view. Second, “high duration” growth stocks could lose out, since so much of these stocks' value is priced based on far-off earnings potential. Higher discount rates for cash flows further in the future would depress the valuations of these stocks. Third, companies that are required to issue long-term debt could be hit harder, since these policy changes would increase borrowing costs for such companies.

Conclusion

The transition from Jerome Powell to Kevin Warsh would likely represent one of the largest regime changes the Fed has seen from a single chairman to his or her successor. The Powell era was defined by pragmatic, “data-driven” gradualism. This framework successfully navigated acute economic shocks, but also led to the central bank’s failure to preempt inflation. The Warsh era (if he’s confirmed) will likely pivot toward policy grounded in mathematical rules, lack of transparent guidance, and reliance on real-time market signals. I’m not convinced that Kevin Warsh is truly the dovish Fed Chairman that President Trump thinks he’s appointing. While Warsh’s “AI thesis” gives him short-term political cover to lower the Fed’s policy rate, that will likely be counteracted by the tightening of the Fed’s balance sheet. In my view, Warsh remains a monetary hawk deep down, and I think that, like he did with Jerome Powell, Trump might have similar buyer’s remorse with Warsh.

Although there may be clear winners and losers from a potential Warsh takeover of the Federal Reserve, I don't think his appointment will temper the broad uncertainty permeating markets right now. The confluence of Middle East war and supply crisis, political uncertainty with midterm elections on the horizon, trade uncertainty, and greater Fed policy uncertainty is likely to continue contributing to choppy returns in 2027 in my view. In a previous article I found a high probability of a market correction before the midterm elections, which we got close to but which hasn't quite occurred yet. I still think there are more buy the dip opportunities ahead this year and so I continue to rate the S&P 500 index and the ETFs that are tracking it as a "Hold" for now.

Thanks for reading and let me know in the comments what you think of the Warsh pick!

data.title: Microsoft And Meta Earnings Previews
data.description:
The strong investment growth in technology infrastructure to support generative AI (GAI) has been a focus over the past year and is projected to expand this year. The expectations for CapEx spending by the hyperscalers continue to move higher. For 2026, consensus year-over-year CapEx is expected to increase by almost $250 billion, from $379 billion to $622 billion. The much stronger pace of the expected CapEx to support the technology infrastructure for AI has caused some concern, as the significant ramp in spending is projected to grow faster than revenues. Will Meta Platforms Inc. (META) and Microsoft Corp. (MSFT) maintain their capex guidance this year?

Microsoft

According to Visible Alpha consensus, total revenues expected for Q3 have remained stable since late October 2025, driven by a resilient view of its core business segments. However, the new Azure AI Services segment is projected to increase for FY 2026, with consensus estimates now expecting $23.4 billion, up from $18.8 billion last January. Expectations for this segment in Q3 2025 have edged up since October and nearly 25% since early 2025. Profitability is also expected to remain resilient for the Company, driving EPS expectations for the quarter and full year up.

We are closely watching what the company will say about the outlook for Azure and OpenAI, as Microsoft’s FY 2026 CapEx numbers have continued to increase steadily. According to consensus projections, CapEx estimates are expected to double from $65 billion in FY 2025 to currently $130 billion in FY 2027.

Microsoft stock has traded down 21% since the last earnings release. The consensus P/E for 2027 has come down from 31x in July and is now 22x. Could the Q3 release and outlook begin to drive outperformance in the stock?

Meta Platforms

According to Visible Alpha consensus, total revenues for Q1 are expected to be $55.5 billion, driven by solid performance in the Family of Apps segment, especially in the US and Europe. Operating profit is expected to be $19.4 billion, driven by steady profitability in the Family of Apps and consensus losses remaining around -$5.0 billion for Reality Labs.

For 2026, earnings expectations for operating income from the Family of Apps have increased $1.0 billion to $108.7 billion, but down from $110.1 billion in the fall, driven by slightly shifting views about ad revenue per DAU in the US and internationally. The management commentary on the outlook in the earnings call will be key to assessing the potential direction of revisions for 2026. In addition, the projected losses from Reality Labs for 2025 have remained and recently ticked back up for 2026 to -$21.8 billion, driven by continued anticipated losses in the division.

In Q1 2025, CEO Mark Zuckerberg highlighted that the company planned to invest in servers and data centers to support AI. CapEx consensus of nearly $125 billion for FY 2026 is a year-over-year increase of $55 billion from FY 2025. There are questions about whether Meta will maintain their CapEx this year.

META stock has been underperforming since last October and is down 10.9%. The consensus P/E for FY 2027 dropped to 19x from 21x last year. Will Meta be able to deliver a profit surprise in Q1 2026 and begin to outperform?

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Mobileye Is An Overlooked Physical AI Play
data.description:
As you can see in the chart below, Mobileye Global Inc. (MBLY) has seen nearly a third of its stock value wiped out in the past 52 weeks. That's after the post-earnings surge that is happening as I write this, and so it is clear that there has been an extended period of weakness. The company is really involved with an industry that's quite promising. Autonomous vehicles are widely considered the future of transportation, and so judging by the stock performance, it seems Mobileye hasn't executed that well. Today, I'm initiating coverage with an earnings review with the aim that by the end of this article there will be a clear investment thesis.

In this article, it is shown that Q1 was a strong quarter overall for the company. While revised full-year guidance is still quite soft, the long-term positioning seems to be strong. Their push into robotaxis is ambitious, and their acquisition of Mentee Robotics could be a long-term game changer as physical AI becomes increasingly important. While the multiyear low level in the valuation could potentially be justified by the weak near-term outlook, I believe the market is overlooking the opportunities that the company has to expand. Therefore, I'm initiating Mobileye at a buy rating.

The Company At A Glance

Before we jump into the results for their 2026 Q1, it is important to note that the company has come quite a long way and that, looking forward, the opportunity seems to be significant.

As you can see in the above infographic, the adoption of the company's autonomous solutions has been quite respectable, as more than 230 million vehicles have been deployed with their SoCs through 2025 Q3. Therefore, while we're not at widespread full autonomy yet, the company really has made an impact on the driving experience for many.

Looking forward, it is shown above that their pipeline offers them opportunity for growth as the market for autonomous solutions and physical AI expands in the coming years. The opportunity is there, and so the key for their success from here onwards is probably execution.

Back To Growth

For 2026 Q1, Mobileye generated revenues of $558 million, up 27% YoY. That is fairly respectable top-line growth, especially when considering the 9% drop that was experienced in the previous quarter. They cited a ramp-up in EyeQ SoC volumes, and so at this point it seems that business activity has hit a bit of an inflection point. They also beat revenue expectations by $38.44 million to show outperformance, and so despite what the 52-week stock performance suggests, Mobileye's business trajectory doesn't seem all that bad right now.

Margins Are Mixed

While their revenue growth bounced back into positive territory, their sales mix had an effect on their adjusted gross margin, as there was a 241 basis point contraction. In their earnings release, they stated that "this was mainly due to a higher EyeQ-related cost per unit given the different mix of EyeQ products sold." While that may not be the best news, Mobileye was able to more than offset that with improved expense control.

For Q1, the company still managed a 360 basis point expansion for adjusted operating margin to reflect that profitability as a whole still remains in a good place. As you can see in the chart above, they reported an adjusted EPS of $0.12, which is a nice 51% jump from the year-ago quarter. They also beat expectations by $0.03, and so at this point I believe the shakiness in their gross margin isn't a major concern for investors.

Full Year Guidance Raised

After beating on the top and bottom lines for Q1, the company raised their full-year guidance for both revenue and adjusted operating income. They now expect revenues to be in the range of $1.935 billion to $2.015 billion, and at the new midpoint, they are hinting at top-line growth of 4%. This would still be a deceleration from 2025, and so despite the bounce back in growth for Q1, it seems the near-term outlook isn't overly rosy at this point.

As for adjusted operating income, they see the figure being between $185 million and $235 million. At the midpoint of this range, they are implying a 25% decline, and so profitability will deteriorate moving forward. This is quite a disappointment after the operating margin expansion in Q1. Despite the rise of guidance, I would say that the near-term outlook remains soft. The stock weakness in recent months is probably mainly driven by the previously even softer guidance.

Opportunities

One of the largest opportunities for Mobileye moving forward would be the MOIA Robotaxi that they are developing with Volkswagen Group (VLKAF). As you can see in the infographic above, they aim to be driverless in the United States by 2026 Q4, and commercial rollout will start next year. The fact that they are partnering with Uber (UBER) is another good sign, as it could mean that adoption will gain steam more quickly.

As of now, the target for MOIA is 100,000 Robotaxis on the road by 2033, and so it should be clear that there's opportunity in this space for Mobileye. Looking further ahead, upcoming technologies, like their EyeQ7H, will help to support mind-off levels of autonomy and power the next generation of robotaxis.

Beyond autonomous vehicles, physical AI is clearly becoming an important market, and so the company's move into robotics seems like a justified move. Earlier this year, they announced their acquisition of Mentee Robotics, and in Q1, that deal closed. While immediate revenue benefits will be extremely limited, I believe this acquisition helps to position the company well over the long run.

Obviously a lot has to go right over the next few years, but the above timeline clearly shows that they are ambitious and that there will ultimately be commercial value in their efforts. Within the robotics space, humanoids are perhaps the most exciting, and so I believe Mobileye has correctly acquired a company that has demonstrated innovation with their Mentee Bot.

Fierce Competition Is A Risk

With Mobileye operating in an industry that's expanding rapidly, the largest risk that the company faces is probably fierce competition. The massive list of companies showing off self-driving innovations at CES clearly shows that this is the case. For example, Forbes reported that Nvidia (NVDA) announced a new self-driving stack called Alpamayo, and Alphabet's (GOOGL) Waymo was also present at the tech event.

In addition to autonomous vehicles, it should also be noted that the robotics space has no shortage of competition either. There are many startups out there, and Elon Musk's Tesla (TSLA) is also set to introduce Optimus in the not-so-distant future. At the end of the day, the opportunity is there, and now it's just how well Mobileye can execute moving forward.

Valuation Analysis

While a forward P/E in the low thirties isn't particularly low even when compared to the tech sector, I would say that the near multiyear low level currently presents patient investors with a good amount of opportunity. Furthermore, the company's announcement of a $250 million share buyback program is a signal that management has confidence in their stock's value.

Today, it was shown that Q4 was a robust quarter. Revenues grew significantly, and the bottom line saw strength even though gross margin was a little shaky. While full-year guidance was raised, the near-term outlook was still shown to be disappointing. Even though that may help to justify the multiple contractions that have been going on, I would say that this short-termism misses the mark. The company is facing strong competition on many fronts, but I would say that the autonomous vehicle and physical AI market is large enough for many to succeed. Furthermore, given the company's longstanding leadership in this space, I believe they have the expertise and innovation that should help them to capture a decent part of the market.

With that being the case, investors may have grown relatively too pessimistic on the company's prospects. There seem to be near-term challenges, but the long-term positioning of the company shows that the forward P/E doesn't deserve to be down near multiyear lows. If the market viewed the company as a humanoid robotic startup, there is little question that the valuation would be much higher than it is right now.

Takeaway

Mobileye spun off from Intel (INTC) a few years ago, and by now it seems that all the buzz has died down. I believe this presents investors with a good opportunity to buy the stock for a historically cheap price. With their move into robotaxis and robotics, the company has quietly become a physical AI leader. As global adoption continues over the coming years or even decades, they're in a position to benefit from the tailwinds even if competition is a potential risk. In my view, the long term prospects for Mobileye are probably as strong as they have ever been, and I have decided to initiate the stock at a buy rating.

data.title: The Jobs Market Is Not Okay
data.description:
The monthly jobs report from the Bureau of Labor Statistics will come out in two weeks. Do you want to hazard a guess as to the number of nonfarm payroll (NFP) gains reported for the month of April? Good luck with that – see below.

In the past fifteen months we have had nine months of payroll gains and six months of payroll losses, according to the BLS data. And this chart doesn’t reflect an even more bizarre facet of the BLS report, in which initial estimates are subject to sweeping revisions.

Case in point: the initial report for February 2026 had nonfarm payrolls declining by 92,000. A few weeks later, that number was revised to payroll losses totaling 133,000. Who knows what that March NFP figure of 178,000 gains will look like when the BLS gives us a revision in its next release on May 8?

Below the Headlines

All the payrolls volatility notwithstanding, the headline numbers themselves are not flashing five-alarm warnings. The unemployment rate, which has bounced around between 4.0 and 4.5 percent since the beginning of last year, is nowhere close to the levels normally associated with economic downturns (though that can change quickly when exogenous factors impact prior assumptions about growth trends).

At least one industry sector – healthcare – is a reliable enough job creation machine to offset steadier losses in other sectors like manufacturing and transportation services.

Below the headlines, though, there are plenty of indications that things are moving in the wrong direction. Fed Chair Jay Powell is of the opinion that net job creation is effectively zero, which does not look too different from that chart above showing nearly offsetting NFP gains and losses from month to month.

The situation for entry-level jobs is even worse. The unemployment rate for recent college graduates (ages 22-27) is around 5.6 percent, higher than the overall figure of 4.3 percent and well above the 3.1 percent level for all college graduates.

Anecdotal evidence from young job seekers paints a hellish picture of thousands of resumes and cover letters sent into the void of employment search platforms, never to be heard from again. Graduate degrees in areas once thought to be sure-fire recipes for successful careers are not the golden ticket that was supposed to be worth that $100,000-plus investment in one’s education.

The Specter of AI

Looming over all the present uncertainty in the labor market is the specter of artificial intelligence. Don’t take it from us – listen to what the experts in the field themselves have to say.

Dario Amodei, the CEO of Anthropic (ANTHRO), opined recently that roughly 50 percent of entry-level white-collar jobs are at risk of disappearing within a one to five-year time frame, potentially creating an unemployment spike as high as 20 percent.

The most exposed industries, according to Amodei, are those very ones that attract the brightest and most ambitious cohort of young people – finance, consulting, law and tech.

OpenAI (OPENAI), Anthropic’s peer and rival in the AI space, projects that 18 percent of all jobs will soon be automated with AI capabilities. A recent CNBC study found that AI-related layoffs totaled 55,000 in 2025. Every day we see evidence of major layoffs at tech firms – this week it was Meta (META) (10 percent reduction to “offset” spend on AI) and Microsoft (MSFT) (voluntary redundancy of 7 percent) in the headlines.

Of course, for every AI Cassandra warning of the crisis ahead, there is a Pollyanna pointing to the long history of doomsaying at the dawn of new technologies that proves overblown. This time, though, there may be more reason to pay attention to the Cassandras than to blithely assume that new doors will open for each one shut by the incursion of AI.

The technology is growing and spreading and becoming more sophisticated at a mind-blowing rate. Not just here at home, either. China’s DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) platform, which caused a major freak-out in the AI space early last year when it debuted, appears to be closing in on the capabilities of the leading US models.

So there is a geopolitical element overlaid on what could be one of the biggest macroeconomic shake-ups ever experienced in our country. Regulators, policymakers and business leaders need to be ready to take up the challenge. As do the rest of us.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Flash PMIs Highlight Growing Stagflation Risks Among The Major Developed Economies
data.description:
S&P Global’s flash PMI data showed output growth continuing to run at only a very modest rate across the major developed economies in April, as the war in the Middle East dampened demand.

Hardest hit have been the service sectors, which collectedly reported the first drop in demand since late 2023. While manufacturing output growth accelerated, the improvement was buoyed by firms building safety stocks for items facing supply availability issues or likely to rise in price.

Supply delays are already their most widespread since 2022 in all major developed economies, while prices are also reported to have been rising at sharply increased rates, likely in all cases to feed through to marked accelerations in consumer price inflation.

The combination of falling demand and surging price growth points to the growing risk of stagflation in the major developed economies, in turn presenting dilemmas for central banks.

While high inflation generally warrants higher interest rates to dampen demand, sluggish growth generally warrants lower interest rates to stimulate demand.

Developed economies report falling demand as war impact hits

Business activity growth across the four largest developed economies – the US, eurozone, Japan and UK, the ‘G4’ – continued to grow in April, but at only a very modest pace, constrained by the first drop in new business placed at companies for the first time since 2023.

Since the outbreak of war at the end of February, S&P Global’s PMI output index for the G4 economies has averaged just 50.8 (a reading of 50.7 in March was followed by a flash estimate of 50.9 in April), signalling only very modest growth and down from an average of 52.4 in the opening two months of the year. The past two months have therefore seen the weakest growth spell since the start of 2024.

The G4 PMI new orders index, meanwhile, fell below the 50.0 no-change level in April for the first time since December 2023 to indicate a marginal drop in aggregate demand for goods and services.

Worst hit by the war of the G4 economies has been the eurozone, where output fell in April for the first time since late 2024. Eurozone new orders fell for a second month running, dropping at the fastest rate since November 2024.

Both the US and UK, meanwhile, reported modest rebounds in output from near-stalled positions in March, though in both cases growth remained well below rates seen at the start of the year thanks to very subdued order book growth.

While Japan reported the strongest increase in output of the G4, its growth rate faded for a second successive month to the slowest since December.

Service sector hardest hit

The principal area of weakness was the service sector, where growth across the G4 remained largely stalled in April, having slowed sharply in March. Inflows of new business into the G4 services economies fell for the first time since November 2023.

Service sector activity in the eurozone contracted at the fastest rate since the COVID-19 lockdowns in early 2021, while only modest gains were seen in both the US and Japan. A more substantial rise was seen in the UK, though even here the rate of services growth fell well short of that recorded earlier in the year.

In contrast, manufacturing output growth accelerated across each G4 economy, rising in aggregate at the fastest rate since August 2021, during the pandemic. However, as seen in the pandemic, factory output growth has been boosted since the outbreak of war by stock building among producers and their customers, reflecting an eagerness to either prepare for supply shortages or to build stock before prices rise further.

Supply delays at highest since 2022

Concerns over supply shortages were highlighted by supplier delivery times lengthening across the G4 on average to the greatest extent since July 2022. The incidence of delays spiked higher in all four economies to an extent not seen since 2022, though the highest incidence was reported in the UK, followed by Japan. The latter was noteworthy in that the extent of delays broadly matched that seen in the early days of the pandemic, reflecting Japan’s relatively greater reliance on shipments through the Strait of Hormuz.

Shortages were reported for fuel and fuel-based products as well as a wide variety of other inputs ranging from foodstuffs to chemicals and plastics to metals.

Prices spike higher

Delivery delays are commonly linked to upward price pressures (read more about the PMI Suppliers’ Delivery Times Index here), and input prices rose sharply as a result in the spike in supply shortages seen in April, rising in manufacturing across the G4 at the steepest pace since July 2022.

However, price rises were not confined to manufactured goods, with services input costs across the G4 also rising sharply and at the steepest pace since April 2023.

Upward input cost pressures were reported in all G4 economies, with the UK reporting the steepest rise and the US the slowest.

Higher costs fed through to higher selling prices for both goods and services, with overall rates of inflation climbing sharply higher in all G4 economies. The UK reported the steepest rate of increase, followed by the US, where the impact of the war exacerbated existing tariff-related price pressures.

Higher inflation

In all cases, the PMI data point to marked increases in consumer price inflation in the coming months as war-related price hikes feed through to households.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: TWOD: So What Happens To The Bonds Is The Question
data.description:
Thesis

We recently did an extensive piece on the Two Harbors Investment Corp. (TWO) merger with CrossCountry Mortgage (to be referred to as 'CCM'), where we went through the merger timeline and the background to the transaction. In that article we focused on the impact on the preferred shares, and identified the Series C (TWO.PR.C) as the best way to take advantage of the corporate action. We got a few questions regarding the impact on the bonds from TWO; hence, we are writing this piece for the holders of the Two Harbors Investment Corp. CAL 30 (TWOD).

Recap of what is happening

CrossCountry Mortgage (to be referred to as 'CCM') is a private mortgage lender out of Ohio who has agreed to purchase TWO:

Two Harbors Investment (TWO) on Thursday announced a new definitive merger agreement with CrossCountry Mortgage, terminating its previously announced agreement with UWM Holdings (UWMC). Last week, Two said it received an unsolicited proposal for $10.70 per share in cash. The new proposal provides for the payment of the $25.4M termination fee that would be required under the UWMC agreement. Earlier this week, Two board's ad hoc committee said that CrossCountry Mortgage's unsolicited bid constitutes a "superior" proposal to the merger agreement it had previously reached with UWM Holdings.

For the CCM merger to be complete, the TWO shareholders need to approve it (in the next month it will happen), and then regulatory approvals need to be obtained. That puts the completion date somewhere in the second half of 2026; thus, September 30, 2026, is a good guess as of now. This is important to remember because, from a legal standpoint, the TWOD bonds are not affected until the actual legal date when the merger becomes official.

What does the TWOD prospectus say

Firstly a quick recap of the bond characteristics:

  • Outstanding notional: $100 mm.
  • Coupon: 9.375%.
  • Par value: $25/share.
  • Call date: 5/15/2027.
  • Maturity date: 8/15/2030.
  • Distribution dates: 2/15, 5/15, 8/15 & 11/15.

The first call date is not until May 2027, so we have to explore if the prospectus has any covenants addressing mergers:

A bond covenant is a legally binding agreement between a bond issuer and a bondholder that sets terms meant to protect both sides. Negative covenants limit the issuer from taking certain activities, while affirmative covenants require the issuer to meet specific requirements.

Basically, when you lend to a company via purchasing a bond, you want to ensure the company does not take steps that can negatively impact you down the road. The stricter the covenants for a bond deal, the more protection is assured for a holder. Covenants covering mergers are fairly standard.

Investors can find the TWOD prospectus here. We are going to focus on the following covenant covering the current merger:

The bond contains what is called a 'Change of Control' covenant, which spells out what happens if the company is purchased by a different entity. These sorts of covenants are introduced to ensure that initial bondholders benefit from the same treatment as before if there is a merger.

The bond covenant perfectly addresses the current situation where a private company (CCM) is purchasing TWO. Bondholders now have access to SEC financials as holders of TWOD. Once the merger is consummated, they would no longer have access to the financial standing of the new company, which is an issue. We therefore have the following wording in the prospectus:

“Change of Control Repurchase Event” means that, following the consummation of a Change of Control, neither the Company nor the acquiring or surviving entity has a class of common securities (or American Depositary Receipts representing such securities) listed on the New York Stock Exchange (the “NYSE”), the NYSE American LLC (the “NYSE AMER”) or the Nasdaq Stock Market, or listed or quoted on an exchange or quotation system that is a successor to the NYSE, the NYSE AMER or the Nasdaq Stock Market. Notwithstanding the foregoing, no Change of Control Repurchase Event will be deemed to have occurred in connection with any particular Change of Control unless and until such Change of Control has actually been consummated.

So the covenant specifically addresses today's situation where CCM as a private company does not have any class of common securities listed on the NYSE or the NYSE AMER exchange. This situation triggers a 'Change of Control Repurchase Event':

If a Change of Control Repurchase Event (defined below) occurs, unless we have exercised our option to redeem the notes as described above, we will make an offer to each holder of notes to repurchase all or any part (in a principal amount of $25 and integral multiples of $25 in excess thereof) of that holder’s notes at a repurchase price in cash equal to 101% of the aggregate principal amount of notes repurchased plus any accrued and unpaid interest on the notes repurchased to, but excluding, the date of repurchase

To put all the above in English, because CCM is a private company, the new entity has to offer holders of TWOD a 101% price for their bonds once the merger goes through. Be very careful here - this is not a mandatory redemption, but a mandatory offer for holders. Holders can accept the offer or walk away and still hold the bonds. This provision will be automatically triggered in September 2026 (our estimated completion date for the merger).

What does this all mean for holders, and what should they do?

As a holder of TWOD, you are protected by the above covenant. If you want to sell in September 2026 when the merger consummates, you will have the opportunity to do so at a price of 101%. CCM is rated BB- by Fitch, and its 4-year debt trades at a yield to maturity of roughly 7% currently, so better than TWO. However, the difference is not enormous in terms of costs of funds, and CCM has committed to retiring TWO's preferred equity, which will put strain on its cash balances. The company might do a debt issuance to fund the retirement of the preferred equity and possibly TWOD as well.

TWOD is currently trading with a 9% current yield and is being taken over by an entity with a better cost of funds, although private. Investors who want control over financials and visibility into performance should take the 101% offer from the company (a $25.25/share price on a clean basis). Investors willing to take a chance on CCM will probably wait it out until May 2027, when the company will certainly take the debenture out via cheaper notes. In either case, it is a win for TWOD holders since the credit profile is improving and they are going to be offered a 101% buyout by the new entity.

Conclusion

TWOD are debentures from TWO. While the CCM merger agreement specifically addressed taking out the preferred equity from TWO, it did not mention the bonds. As per the above analysis, the new company will be forced to make a 101% offer to TWOD holders upon the consummation of the merger. Holders who want to keep their position in an unlisted company will very likely be taken out automatically in May 2027 when the first call date occurs for the bonds.
